review_text: Lynch doesn't seem to have read very much. He's only seen every movie ever made and in Blue Velvet goes far to elevate the art, if those are the words for it, to a level of purely sensual, personal obsession. It's a movie-lover's movie, but not much more.
The performances from Hopper and Rossellini are extraordinary.
Unlike most great movies, this one loses some of its power with each subsequent viewing.
If you were asked to close your eyes and visualize an image from the cinematic work of David Lynch, there’s a strong possibility the mental picture you conjured is found somewhere in Blue Velvet.
Lynch’s first noir is also, inevitably, his first meta-noir.

At the risk of being called a sick son of a bitch, I believe it to be one of the most extraordinarily erotic and mesmerising experiences I have ever had in the cinema. Blue Velvet has the power to unsettle and disturb like nothing else.
Lynch pushes audiences into places not everyone wants to go, and Blue Velvet is not a conventional good time at the movies. But it is one of the most brilliant and provocative films of the year.
It's as if Fellini were suddenly taking on the punk world of '50s memorabilia. It makes no sense, but it sticks in your mind. Lynch may be brilliant, but I bet he's a little bonkers, too.

Its skittish humor is a survival device to head off utter desolation. Blue Velvet is also fiercely moral, and that explains its stunning force. Only a moralist can be shocked, and shock, on such a seismic scale.
Lynch's background is in painting, and that's probably why you get the feeling he's creating an abstract work up there on the screen, not dealing with objects, but moods. He's a mood painter.
One of the strangest, most hypnotic American films in ages. It is, in every compelling frame, a movie directed by David Lynch, possessor of the most offbeat sensibility of any current film maker.
Lynch's picture is a muddy mess, bumbling alternatively between infantile ideas, gee-whiz story telling, extreme depictions of sadomasochistic behavior and clumsy satire.
The problem is that Blue Velvet doesn't even follow its own perverse rules of logic. It's a jumble of styles and scenes, none of which follows from, or builds towards, anything.

If you demand that a good film offers a unique vision, that it has an enthralling story and interesting characters, and that it generates challenging ideas and full-blooded emotions, Blue Velvet has it all.

Blue Velvet has just enough story for viewers to convince themselves they're watching a rational movie... Yet it’s the way this story is finally absorbed and dissolved in Lynch’s dreamlike moods that gives the film its haunting undertow.

David Lynch's Blue Velvet is a sustained exercise in disorientation, and if it is not exactly the masterpiece he will make someday (if only to balance the fiasco of Dune), it is certainly startling and full of extraordinary touches.
Like an unsettling dream half-remembered the next morning, Blue Velvet persists, grows, rumbles away in the subconscious.
It utterly redeems Lynch from the debacle of Dune and is that rarest of Hollywood products -- a riveting, complex, layered film with a distinctive and highly vivid directorial signature.
It's a love-it / hate-it proposition, but Blue Velvet is one absolute: proof that there is still intelligence and energy, however perverse, in the American movie. Watch out for Lynch. He's dangerous.

There are great lessons to be learned from examining the darker side of human nature. But Lynch isn't teaching; he's exploiting the gruesomely fascinating in the same way that freaks are exploited in sideshows.

As perfect and perfectly disturbing a picture as the American cinema has delivered. This masterful film taps unflinchingly into perversion and decadence in order to present wholesome enlightenment with both a challenge and a conduit.
An amazing, bravura piece of film-making from the surreal mind of writer-director David Lynch.
The film does what it set out to well, but that is not something everyone will appreciate.
It is harrowing, repulsive, accursed, probably clinically deranged, absolutely limned with genius. It is quite extraordinary.

Lynch drags us into a netherworld of darkness, perversion and ugliness, snuffing with brutality whatever intellectual interest we might have in his premise. He insists on rubbing our noses in the stench long past when his point is made.
Blue Velvet is bruising, but it's fascinating -- a bizarre thriller, deliriously erotic, perversely funny, appallingly cruel.

At a time when Hollywood is just a glamorous assembly line, churning out the cinematic equivalent of designer jeans, Blue Velvet hits us between the eyes like a well-swung pool cue in a barroom brawl.
Disconcerting, disagreeable and at times disgusting, Blue Velvet is nonetheless a mesmerizing piece of work that challenges one's notions of what cinema can be.
The final brilliant twist of this extraordinary film is to show us, not just strangeness of what surrounds normal life, but the strangeness of normal life itself.
Blue Velvet is extraordinary, an accomplished exercise in voyeurism which peels back the genteel layers of a nice, refined community to the dirty, turbulent, dark soul beneath.
The film is a playful and disturbing mixture of the real, the surreal and the fantastic, realised through a rich texture of sound and wide-screen images.
When sequels, deals and feature-length MTV musicals are made instead of films, it's rather thrilling to see one that uses the medium as personally as a film of Ford or Welles, Kubrick or Kurosawa.
The combination of sexual primitivism and art-world knowingness brings its own kind of heavy depression. Blue Velvet is a work of art, but it’s not an exhilarating experience.
Say this for Lynch: He does make you sit up and take notice. He also has some strong ideas kicking around in his exploration of ugly, behind-the-scenes doings in a small American town.
Astonishing and so powerful it makes most other movies look like hack jobs, David Lynch's Blue Velvet leaves you limp as a wet wash rag and yet euphoric over the greatness you've just witnessed.

A biliously ironic assault on the goopy fantasies with which movies have traditionally solaced those hard-pressed souls enduring either provincialism or adolescence or (worst-case scenario) both at the same time.
Disturbing, bizarre, repulsive, pointless, vulgar, perverse and as dull as a recycled homily by a jaded church minister.
Surely, this is one of the strangest and visually repellent big-budget films ever made. And yet, some scenes are almost literally unforgettable.

Whatever you may think of this funny, repellent, nightmarish and incredibly original film, Lynch has guts and vision, two qualities as rare in today's Hollywood as good corned beef is in today's Dayton.

It is obscene, horrific, weird, exciting, funny and finally delightful in an entirely perverse way. It's like film noir throwing a party for "High School Confidential" with the catering done by Dalí and Buñuel.
It is a tribute to Lynch's skill that he has created a background which looks real yet is strangely unsettling at the same time.

It gets into areas of dread and shock and torment that are so profoundly dislocating and dreamlike that, watching it, you feel as if you're coiled right up inside the vision of an artist with a live feed to the unconscious.
There is no disputing that Blue Velvet is a well-made film, despite some carelessness in the tangled plot. It has a style and wit that some audiences will be happy to mistake for art.

Ragged performances and overwritten dialogue weaken some scenes, and much of the evil behavior is so obsessively bleak that it seems more tortured than revealing. Still, at times there are vivid glimpses of [David Lynch's] visionary talent.

Blue Velvet finally established Lynch as one of our most promising and original talents. It's easy to see why someone couldn't resist the temptation to label Blue Velvet a masterpiece: It does nothing less than alter our way of seeing.

Extremes have been reached here. Blue Velvet, to put it plainly, is probably the weirdest most audacious English-language movie ever released into the American mainstream -- although it has not been so much released as let loose.
It's perversely original, because Lynch has no use for good taste. He's a sophisticated bull in the china shop of film, using the horror genre here to show us something awfully real.
If you are the parent of a clean-cut teen-ager, however, go see the movie. Your dreams may never be the same.

Hopper's performance won him the prize for the Best Actor at the [Montreal World Film Festival]. His relentless posturing is so extreme, however, some might be more inclined to give him the Grand Prix for over-acting.
This might sound like the stuff of good potboilers are made of. But this is not mere melodrama. Lynch has that talent to disturb epitomised by Hitchcock.
